metric_value: 5 principles: quick upfront planning, sprint teams of ≤7, progress reviews at sprint end, done when functional+quality objectives met or budget/time runs out, subsequent versions not just patches
notes: ADM SCRUM five principles as defined in 1995 Product Management Facility description

metric_value: 3 phases: Planning and System Architecture, Sprints (multiple), Closure
notes: SCRUM three distinct phases as described in 1995 ADM methodology documentation

metric_value: 1-7 members; full staffing includes developer, QA person, documentation member
notes: Ideal Sprint team composition per ADM 1995 SCRUM definition

metric_value: 1 to 6 weeks per Sprint
notes: ADM 1995 Sprint duration; modern Scrum standardized at 1-4 weeks

metric_value: 400,000 beta users before completion
notes: Microsoft Windows 95 beta program used as flagship ISV example of iterative user-involved development

metric_value: fixing mistake after release costs 10-100x the cost of identifying during planning
notes: Classic waterfall justification for heavy upfront planning; Aberdeen contextualizes as based on slower technology cycles

metric_name: Enterprise IS vs ISV development priorities
metric_value: Enterprise IS prioritizes cost, compatibility, functionality, quality; ISVs prioritize time-to-launch, functionality, personnel, quality (cost not primary)
notes: Aberdeen documents fundamental difference in development priority frameworks between IS and ISVs

metric_value: 4 modules: Process Engineer, Planner, Developer, Product Management Facility (new for SCRUM)
notes: MATE flagship product gained Product Management Facility module for SCRUM backlog/risk/issue management

metric_value: IS organizations must adopt ISV-style rapid development or face growing frustration with management demands
notes: Aberdeen predicts SCRUM-type methods are necessary survival strategy for enterprise IS

metric_value: SCRUM will help IS unlock OOP potential; most IS orgs still not planning to use OOP for mission-critical apps
notes: Aberdeen uses OOP unlocking as additional SCRUM benefit; OOP adoption in enterprise was still nascent in 1995

metric_name: Methodologies like SCRUM not just passing fad
metric_value: SCRUM is not a passing fad or one-shot fix; ISVs using it for sustained competitive advantage
notes: Aberdeen predicts SCRUM as durable methodology for sustained competitive advantage

metric_value: Agile Manifesto (February 2001) codified SCRUM-aligned principles; SCRUM became one of most-adopted Agile frameworks globally
notes: Sutherland and Schwaber were Agile Manifesto signatories; SCRUM Guide standardized framework. Aberdeen prediction about SCRUM durability proved exactly right.

metric_value: Waterfall largely displaced by Agile/SCRUM for software development; remains in regulated industries only
notes: Aberdeen 1995 prediction about waterfall inadequacy proved correct; Agile became dominant by 2005-2010

metric_value: SCRUM/Agile adopted by majority of software development teams globally; Scrum.org holds ADM trademarks
notes: Scrum.org now holds ADM trademark portfolio; SCRUM became global standard exactly as Aberdeen predicted

metric_value: SCRUM named for rugby action (forwards quickly move ball via team skill/strength); not an acronym
notes: ADM chose name SCRUM to communicate team effort, intensity, focus—not an acronym

metric_value: Backlog: approved modifications from prior Sprint assigned to teams at start of next Sprint period
notes: Backlog concept as defined in 1995 ADM SCRUM; now central to modern Scrum practice

metric_value: adopting SCRUM would be radical departure from traditional methods for most in-house enterprise development groups
notes: Aberdeen acknowledges SCRUM adoption challenge; accurately predicts significant organizational change required

metric_value: Sprint review includes teams, project manager, customers/prospects, enterprise senior executives
notes: Cross-functional Sprint review as defined in ADM 1995 SCRUM; maps to modern Sprint Review ceremony

metric_value: OO tools required for SCRUM to be more than theoretical vision; tools that didn't exist even one year ago
notes: Aberdeen identifies OO tool availability as enabling condition for SCRUM practical viability

metric_value: Mainframe app suppliers failed transition to Unix/GUI; DOS app suppliers failed Windows 3 transition—inability to change fast enough
notes: Aberdeen uses ISV failure history to motivate SCRUM urgency for enterprise IS

metric_value: Booch, Rumbaugh, Jacobson created Unify Metamodel underpinning SCRUM intellectual basis
notes: Aberdeen cites Three Amigos UML creators as intellectual foundation of SCRUM methodology

metric_name: OS/2 to Windows NT example of mid-Sprint change
metric_value: Mid-Sprint platform change (OS/2 to Windows NT Client) assigned to teams at next Sprint start—SCRUM flexibility demonstrated
notes: Real-world 1995 platform migration used to illustrate SCRUM backlog flexibility

metric_value: Burlington, Massachusetts
notes: ADM location in December 1995 study

metric_value: Volume 8/Number 17, December 7, 1995
notes: Aberdeen Practice Viewpoint v8n17, December 1995

metric_value: New technology not proprietary—competitors use same tools; SCRUM/speed-to-deliver is the competitive differentiator
notes: Aberdeen final conclusion: technology parity means development speed/methodology is competitive differentiator
